title: Reflections
subtitle: Thoughts formed between input and output.
intro: These represent only my personal attempts at organizing thought. Whether you agree or not, you are welcome to read them in a spirit of looking for inspiration and discussion.
They will keep iterating as I continue to exist.
footnote: * About the categories: ‘human’ and ‘animal’ are separated here for structural clarity, not because I see humans as above other animals.
hint: Click anywhere to view the content

items.subtitle: Instinct
items.text:
Human nature is not survival, but the avoidance of pain.

The reason people survive is only that the problems brought by not surviving are painful. People struggle to stay alive in order not to suffer—from hunger, illness, extreme weather, injury.

This also explains why people commit suicide. If human instinct were survival, then no matter what, people would not do it. It is precisely because the pain of living can exceed the pain brought by death that a person chooses death in order to stop bearing that pain.

items.subtitle: Adaptability
items.text: Human instinct is to seek benefit and avoid harm. At the same time, people are adaptive. Desire is never fully satisfied, and pain can distort one’s nature.

Desire is often not satisfied simply by being fulfilled. Many times it is like the yam porridge in Rashomon: the obsession lies in desire itself.

After realizing this, one should stop remaining loyal to desire. Sometimes desire leads toward destruction—toward trampling oneself, and trampling others.

The meaning of life lies in the process; the result is only one form that the process takes.

To overfocus on result-points and pin ‘happiness’ on them is hopeless. To hold the wrong expectation that results will bring happiness is like dangling an apple directly in front of a donkey.

After the Wenchuan earthquake, people rescued from underground had to have their eyes covered immediately, because their eyes were no longer adapted to sunlight.

And if a person has grown up in a distorted environment, that set of defense mechanisms no longer fits in a healthy one. That is the kind of person who seems out of place, who strikes others as strange.

items.subtitle: My Choice
items.text:
Four years ago, I was still full of hatred toward rapists and so many people in society who made me feel suffocated just by looking at them. I thought that, if I was going to kill myself, I would build a group that pretended to lure in adults seeking to rape minors, and then xx everyone who showed up.

But as I kept planning it, I felt deeply dissatisfied. It seemed like that would only destroy one group of people. So I began to think about whether I could build a meaning greater than this pain.

As I thought, I realized two things: first, only by staying alive can something continue without end. Second, many lives in pain still have not chosen to give up, and yet remain helpless.

So I stopped living for myself and began living for life. The original intention of that meaning was to make other lives happier, but it gradually became a kind of inner drive that belonged to me and pulled me out of my own dead end.

From today on, my meaning lies in today, tomorrow, and the future—in what kind of person I want to become, and what kind of things I want to do. I will no longer keep thinking about yesterday and the past; they cannot define what kind of person I am.

One day, I forgave myself.
I once took revenge on someone, but I did not feel happy either.

items.subtitle: Feeling and Reason
items.text: Feeling is the fuel of a person; reason determines where that fuel flows. It is not right to accuse reason of being cold, and the intellectual arrogance that sees emotion as stupid is another form of prejudice.

items.subtitle: Self-Consistency
items.text:
At the core is being able to live coherently in this world. That means having a place in the world where one can stay at ease, ideals one can keep advancing, and the ability to support oneself.

Not arrogant, not avoidant, not trampling others, and not trampling oneself.

items.subtitle: Constructive Thinking
items.text:
If I see a circle that is actually full of bumps and dents, I will feel disappointed. But I know there is no truly perfect circle in this world, only irregular, rough shapes.

Then there is so much I can do: place a chair at a corner, put a skateboard beside a slope.

As long as a real person can use it, I feel very happy and fulfilled.
Find a crack, and do one small thing. — Yixi
If one day I die unexpectedly, I hope the thoughts and things I leave behind will give meaning to other lives.

items.subtitle: Five Freedoms
items.text:
The most basic principle of modern animal welfare: animals under human care should have—

• freedom from hunger and thirst
• freedom of comfort
• freedom from pain, injury, and disease
• freedom from fear and distress
• freedom to express natural behavior

items.subtitle: How I See Other People
items.text: I do not despise or look down on anyone. I think everyone is simply walking a different road.

If a person loves me, or if a person hates me, both are forms of desire. Whether love comes from understanding or desire, and whether hatred is partial or complete, depends on character.

A love grounded in understanding, or a complete hatred, I will both respect and appreciate; a love grounded in desire, or a partial hatred, I will filter out.
I no longer hate anyone. I may approve or disapprove of actions, but I will not elevate that approval or disapproval to the level of an entire person.

items.subtitle: Don’t Presume
items.text:
A classmate was bitten by mosquitoes. I felt worried when I saw it and kept helping to drive them away. They refused twice and explained that it actually did not itch, but even after being refused I kept shooing the mosquitoes away for a while.

I think I should learn to understand other people’s refusals, instead of only doing what looks kind from my side without hearing what they are saying. I should not presume.

When caring for someone, you cannot presume. You cannot be overly soft-hearted, and you cannot be inexplicably forceful. You have to really understand the needs of a life first, and then do what corresponds to them.

items.subtitle: To Be Added
items.text:

• Rescue methods
• What I have learned from keeping snakes

items.subtitle: To Be Added
items.text: The relationship between humans and animals...
Life: the product of consciousness and subjective agency. I will take responsibility for any life I can see.
The meaning of life does not need to be grand. The moment life has subjective agency, it already has meaning. I love all life.
A community with a shared future for humankind—yes, this concept matters a lot to me. The optimal game-theoretic solution is idealistic, but that does not stop me from contributing to it.
